title: Today's AI Brief: 100 things we announced at I/O 2026
summary: A rundown of top AI announcements from I/O 2026, including Google's 100 announcements and Ramp's use of Codex for code review.

body.text: First up, Google's I/O conference was a showcase of their AI prowess. They unveiled 100 new announcements, which is essentially a small army of updates and new features. It's clear they're pushing hard to stay ahead in the AI game. One of the more interesting announcements is the expansion of their model capabilities, which promises to make their AI even more capable and integrated into our daily lives.

Next, I'm looking at how Ramp engineers are using Codex with GPT-5.5 to supercharge their code review process. Imagine getting feedback on your code in minutes instead of hours. That's what Ramp is doing, and it's a great example of how AI can make a real difference in the workflow. No more tedious code reviews; just get the feedback you need and move on.

100 things we announced at I/O 2026 is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: 100 things we announced at I/O 2026 May 20, 2026 · Share x.com Facebook LinkedIn Mail Copy link We've been busy! Here’s a rundown of the top announcements, launches and demos at I/O 2026. Keyword Team Share x.com Facebook LinkedIn Mail Copy link This week at Google I/O 2026, we unveiled new models, agents and tools to help you build, search, create, discove... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

How Ramp engineers accelerate code review with Codex is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: How Ramp engineers use Codex with GPT-5.5 to review code and ship improvements, allowing them to get substantive feedback in minutes instead of hours. The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.

sources.title: How Ramp engineers accelerate code review with Codex
